TrackFormer · every forecast, on the map

v10 vs v21 vs v23 — four held-out storms

Storms Bavi, Wayne, Co-may and Hinnamnor, every full-horizon forecast produced — 786 forecasts in all. v10 no environmental field at all; v21 chain-of-thought: predicts the steering flow, derives the track; v23 v21 + temporal steering stack: t-24 h, t-12 h and now. Each model gets its own panel so overlapping tracks never have to be told apart by colour alone.

one forecastmean by valid timeobserved
2026

Bavi

Every full-horizon forecast this storm produced — 49 of them — drawn as one thin line each. Bold is the mean by valid time; Dotted black is the observed track from genesis.

1986

Wayne

Every full-horizon forecast this storm produced — 121 of them — drawn as one thin line each. Bold is the mean by valid time; Dotted black is the observed track from genesis.

2025

Co-may

Every full-horizon forecast this storm produced — 43 of them — drawn as one thin line each. Bold is the mean by valid time; Dotted black is the observed track from genesis.

2022

Hinnamnor

Every full-horizon forecast this storm produced — 49 of them — drawn as one thin line each. Bold is the mean by valid time; Dotted black is the observed track from genesis.

Coastlines are Natural Earth 50 m land polygons, simplified to the resolution each panel can actually show. Projection is equirectangular with longitude scaled by cos(latitude), so shapes are locally true at the centre of each panel.

The bold line is the mean by valid time, not a weighted consensus. At each moment it averages the positions every forecast assigns to that moment, with no lead-weighting and no smoothing. Bins holding fewer than three forecasts are dropped — at the ends of a storm's life only one initialisation reaches that far, and a one-member “mean” is just that single forecast drawn in bold.

The mean is averaged by valid time, not by lead. Forecasts launched hours apart only describe the same moment at different lead offsets — averaging by lead would blend positions hours apart and draw a route no model ever predicted.

A mean over all launches is not a forecast you could have issued. It includes initialisations only six hours before the moment they describe, so its error is not comparable to the 120 h figure quoted in each caption. The captions are the honest forecast numbers; the bold line only shows the centre of the spread.

The observed track is reconstructed from the base position of every window, which is an actual best-track fix, so it is data rather than a fit.
